results.title: ISDN
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Two-channel video

Duration: 523 days 17 hours 52 minutes

Courtesy of the artist, Victoria Miro, and David Zwirner
results.description:
Two screens face each other. You are caught in a call-and-response cypher unfolding across continents. In London, grime artists Lady Sanity and TrueMendous exchange bars. In Cairo, mahraganat musicians Raptor and Joker respond. Each delivers freestyle verses that question race and class in their home countries.

While researching the global uprisings of 2011, Douglas uncovered accounts of grime being played at Occupy protests in the UK. Looking for a parallel in North Africa, he identified mahraganat – an Egyptian street music blending hip hop, electronic beats and shaabi (popular music) – as a similar local form. Both genres come from marginalised communities, shaped by local language, rhythm and political urgency. Their pairing connects music scenes forged in protest and public space.

The exchange feels live, but the performances were recorded weeks apart. Through editing, synchronisation and shared tempo, Canadian artist Stan Douglas creates the illusion of real time. The title refers to Integrated Services Digital Network, once used to transmit sound across distance. Here, technology links communities and shapes how global culture is heard and experienced.
description_for_label:

results.title: bausatz noto
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Record player, vinyl records with locked grooves, mixing board, amplifier

Co-commissioned by The Vinyl Factory
results.description:
In bausatz noto, Carsten Nicolai, founder of the experimental label Noton, explores sound as a physical system rather than a finished composition. Using turntables, vinyl records and locked grooves, he focuses on repetition, duration and subtle variation.

Each interaction produces a different result, so no version is ever exactly the same. Private listening becomes shared experience, shaped by technology and by the choices of those who play it.

How to play

This table is an instrument. Each turntable plays a vinyl record cut with locked grooves that repeat in short loops. These loops produce brief sound fragments that can be combined to produce infinitely new compositions.

You can start and stop the records, change the speed, swap discs, or let them rotate slightly off-centre. Each small adjustment changes how the sounds overlap and combine.

Use the headphones to listen carefully as layers build, repeat and shift. Pay attention to timing, repetition and silence as much as to sound.
description_for_label:

results.title: 100 Records
results.description:
An independent music and arts enterprise, The Vinyl Factory collaborates with artists and musicians to create boundary-pushing audio-visual work. Its pressing plant traces back to the inventor of the gramophone in 1890 and later became part of EMI, producing records by The Beatles, Pink Floyd and Sex Pistols. Today, it continues to operate using the iconic EMI 1400 press, producing over two million records each year.

100 Records presents a selection of limited-edition releases from The Vinyl Factory label. From hand-painted sleeves to live direct-to-disc recordings, each work foregrounds vinyl as both material and social process, capturing moments of exchange where production, artwork and performance intersect. The display also demonstrates that where a record is made shapes how it is heard.

The display reflects vinyl’s resurgence and the infrastructures that sustain it. Record stores, studios, pressing plants and listening spaces support local culture beyond digital algorithms. In Melbourne, independent labels, long-running record stores and community radio keep vinyl active as a shared, in-person experience, linking London’s production history with Melbourne’s ongoing culture of listening.

results.title: 12-Inch Voices
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Plywood, metal, foam, paint, soundsystem

Music by Yussef Dayes

Commissioned by The Vinyl Factory in collaboration with Lafyette Anticipations
results.description:
This giant boombox, wrapped in jagged pink acoustic foam, looks like the walls of an anechoic chamber, a room made to soak up sound. Even so, music still fills the space around it. Co-commissioned by The Vinyl Factory, the sculpture holds a tension between amplification and silence, spectacle and control.

In 12-Inch Voices, artist, architect and designer Virgil Abloh draws on his time as a DJ in Chicago. In house and techno music, rhythm helps shape the music, and repeated sounds become part of its form. His influences ranged from techno pioneer Juan Atkins to the clean lines of architect Mies van der Rohe. Jamaican diasporic sound system culture also influenced his thinking, where custom-built speaker stacks and street dances turn music into a public experience. The track playing here is jazz drummer Yussef Dayes’ Love Is the Message.

Recasting the sound system as sculpture, Abloh brings club culture into the gallery and shows how sound shapes the spaces where people come together.

results.title: BLKNWS ®
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Original Variations, Unique Variant #2

Two channel broadcast

Duration: 26 minutes 31 seconds

Co-commissioned by The Vinyl Factory
results.description:
This news channel doesn’t exist, yet it feels live. Headlines scroll as archival footage cuts to memes, interviews, music videos and breaking updates. The format is familiar, but the perspective isn’t – it’s told through a distinctly Black lens.

Co-commissioned by The Vinyl Factory, BLKNWS® is an ongoing film and installation by artist and filmmaker Kahlil Joseph. Conceived as a new kind of television network, it assembles found footage, news clips, original reporting, popular culture and music into a rolling broadcast. Scenes from the Civil Rights movement sit alongside social media videos, Hollywood films, animation and everyday life. Soul, rap and hip hop set the pace, shaping how images connect and how histories are felt.

Rather than simply representing Black experience, Joseph reworks the structure of the news itself. By layering sound and image, he exposes how stories are framed, edited and repeated – and asks who has the power to define what becomes history.

See a film program curated by Kahlil Joseph in the ACMI Cinemas from DATE to DATE.

results.title: Shelly Belly Inna Real Life
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 23 minutes 52 seconds

Co-commissioned by The Vinyl Factory
results.description:
The film begins with a dancer moving from classical ballet into dancehall, as one style slowly transforms into another. We see how dance travels across cultures, changing as people adapt it and make it their own.

Commissioned by The Vinyl Factory, the work grew from a long collaboration between artist and choreographer Cecilia Bengolea and dancehall communities in Kingston, Spanish Town and Bog Walk in Jamaica. At its centre is Shelly Belly, an influential dancer who guides us through the story.

Dancehall began in Jamaica in the late 1970s and 80s as a faster, more electronic style of reggae. It grew in neighbourhoods facing poverty and political tension, where street dances became places to gather. People came to be seen, compete and express themselves, circling powerful sound systems and moving for hours to heavy bass and heat. These gatherings became spaces of shared release, shaped by music, movement and community.
description_for_label:

results.title: Ijò, Film
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 8 minutes 28 seconds

Music by James William Blades

Commissioned by The Vinyl Factory and 180 Studios
results.description:
Young ballet dancers move through classrooms and open courtyards in Lagos. Their bodies shift between careful practice and playful energy. As they leap and turn, movement, music and image come together to explore identity, belief and belonging.

In Ijó, artist Gabriel Moses follows students from the Leap of Dance Academy in Nigeria. He brings together his Nigerian heritage and his South London upbringing. Classical ballet, often linked to European tradition, is reimagined through the strength and skill of Black dancers. Moments of strict discipline soften into gestures inspired by everyday life and street dance. Christian symbols and hymn-like singing mix with clapping and rhythms rooted in African traditions, in a powerful score by James William Blades.

The film moves between quiet, close moments and larger, shared scenes. Children laugh and practise. Families gather and watch. Through dance, the film shows how movement can carry stories across cultures and generations.
description_for_label:

results.title: The Listening Room
results.description:
This room is designed for focused listening. A high-quality sound system, built with Australian manufacturer Pitt & Giblin, who handcraft high-performance, active loudspeakers, reveals the depth, texture and detail of each recording.

You’re invited to select from a rotating collection of 20 records from The Vinyl Factory. These include releases from the label, alongside works that have shaped its direction – from Ryuichi Sakamoto and Sonic Youth to Grace Jones and Massive Attack. The record selection also responds to Melbourne’s music calendar and will change periodically throughout the exhibition.

After hours, the room becomes a site for live sessions, talks and deep-listening sessions. Selected sessions are curated by Yasmine Sharaf, presenter for Triple R’s Cease + Desist and a specialist in underground sounds.

Take your time, listen closely.

Use your exhibition ticket to enter the ballot for upcoming exclusive listening sessions. Visit the ACMI website via the QR code for a list of upcoming events.

results.title: Untitled (ɔl bɔdi na sta)
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 7 minutes

Music by Julianknxx, anaiis, Thabo, Aron Kyne

Commissioned by The Vinyl Factory and 180 Studios
results.description:
Two people move inside a circle of salt. They scrape the ground, breathe deeply, collide and hold each other up. Their movements feel searching and unsure. The salt shifts under their feet. It becomes a surface to stand on, a sound to hear and a material that changes between safety and struggle.

Untitled (Ol Bodi Na Sta) began when Sierra Leonean artist Julianknxx saw dancers Karel Kouelany and Joël Assebako perform. The experience stayed with him. He returned to it through film, drawing on his background in rap and poetry. The images move like verses in a song, shaped by rhythm, repetition, and pause.

An original score, created with musicians anaiis, Thabo and Aron Kyne, weaves together rhythm and Krio, a language spoken in Sierra Leone. Through sound and movement, the film shows how ritual and memory travel between people. As the bodies move, they ask: how do we carry who we are, together?
description_for_label:

results.title: Second-hand Reading
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 7 minutes

Courtesy of the artist
results.description:
Poetic figures, phrases, abstractions and landscapes surface across printed pages, dissolving, jumping and morphing as they turn. Through charcoal, ink and watercolour, South African artist William Kentridge transforms the pages of Cassell’s Cyclopædia of Mechanics (1914) and the Shorter Oxford English Dictionary (1936). into flip-book animation.

In apartheid South Africa, systems of knowledge were shaped by colonial power and racial hierarchy. Dictionaries and encyclopaedias carried that authority, presenting language as neutral while reinforcing exclusion. By drawing over them, Kentridge changes what the words seem to mean. He shows that language isn’t fixed. It shifts over time and carries ideas from the past.

Kentridge chose a piano and vocal score by Neo Muyanga featuring a Sesotho funeral hymn written in response to the 2012 Marikana massacre, when police killed 34 striking mine workers. The music brings grief, protest and labour struggle into the film, connecting these older printed texts to more recent histories of violence and inequality in South Africa. Drawing, language and song turn second-hand pages into a living surface where history presses against the present.
description_for_label:
